article_title: Tesla Faces March 9 NHTSA Deadline to Hand Over FSD Crash Data as Austin Robotaxi Fleet Crashes at Four Times the Human Rate
summary: Two factual inaccuracies identified in the Analysis section require correction before publication.

content_preview.title: Tesla Faces March 9 NHTSA Deadline to Hand Over FSD Crash Data as Austin Robotaxi Fleet Crashes at Four Times the Human Rate
content_preview.summary: NHTSA gave Tesla a final March 9 deadline to deliver crash video and telemetry from its FSD investigation, while data shows Austin robotaxis crash four times more often than human drivers.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Tesla has until March 9, 2026 to deliver critical crash video, event data recorder files, and CAN bus telemetry to the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration as part of a widening federal investigation into its Full Self-Driving software. The deadline, already extended twice from its original January 19 date, arrives as newly compiled data shows Tesla's Austin robotaxi fleet is experiencing collisions at roughly four times the rate of human drivers by the company's own metri...

recommendations: Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Strong reporting on the core NHTSA investigation and Austin robotaxi crash data. The article is well-structured across Overview, What We Know, What We Don't Know, and Analysis sections. Key statistics are well-attributed to specific sources. Word count (835) is appropriate for the News category.
editor_notes.source_verification: Three of four sources (Electrek x2, Gizmodo) were successfully fetched and verified. The CMU Safety21 source (safety21.cmu.edu) is a legitimate Carnegie Mellon University research center page but its article body renders via JavaScript and could not be fully extracted. The 8,313 records and 300-per-day processing rate claims attributed to it are independently confirmed by multiple other outlets (Mercury News, Irish Times, East Bay Times, Claims Journal). The CMU source should be added to config/source_allowlist.txt.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Core news claims are accurate and well-supported: NHTSA investigation PE25012 with 80 incidents, March 9 deadline after two extensions, 14 Austin robotaxi crashes over ~800,000 miles, 4x human rate per Tesla's own Vehicle Safety Report, 8x rate per NHTSA police-reported standards. Two factual inaccuracies found in the Analysis section: (1) The article states Waymo 'reduces injury-causing crashes by 80 percent compared to human drivers' — the actual published figure is a 90% reduction in serious injury-causing crashes (per Waymo's own 2025 year-in-review). (2) The article states Waymo 'now completes over 450,000 fully driverless rides per week across six cities' — as of February 24, 2026 (ten days before this submission), Waymo expanded to 10 cities. The six-city figure was accurate before that expansion.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: The article is direct and factual through the news sections. The Analysis section is more pointed in contrasting Waymo favorably against Tesla, which is editorially appropriate given the documented safety comparisons, but the two inaccurate figures in that section undermine the credibility of the broader contrast being drawn.
editor_notes.originality: No duplicate coverage found in the existing article archive. Topic is timely and distinct. The article synthesizes multiple concurrent developments (NHTSA deadline, robotaxi crash data, redaction practices) into a coherent narrative.
concerns: FACTUAL ERROR: 'reduces injury-causing crashes by 80 percent' — Waymo's published safety figure is a 90% reduction in serious injury crashes, not 80%.
FACTUAL ERROR: 'across six cities' — Waymo expanded to 10 cities on February 24, 2026, ten days before this submission. The article should cite the current figure of 10 cities.
The CMU Safety21 source (safety21.cmu.edu) is not in the source allowlist and should be evaluated for addition.
recommendations: Correct the Waymo injury reduction figure from '80 percent' to '90 percent' in the Analysis section.
Correct the Waymo city count from 'six cities' to 'ten cities' in the Analysis section.
Add safety21.cmu.edu to config/source_allowlist.txt for future use — it is a legitimate Carnegie Mellon University safety research center.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: The article's core news reporting is solid, accurate, and well-sourced. The NHTSA deadline coverage, crash rate statistics, and investigation details are all verified. However, two identifiable factual errors in the Analysis section — both concerning Waymo comparison figures that can be independently confirmed — must be corrected before publication. These errors are in contextual/comparative passages rather than the core Tesla-NHTSA story, but accuracy standards require correction regardless of where inaccuracies appear.
